author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

In this book Augustin refutes the second letter which Petilianus wrote to him after having seen the first of Augustin’s earlier books. This letter had been full of violent language; and Augustin rather shows that the arguments of Petilianus had been deficient and irrelevant, than brings forward arguments in support of his own statements.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: Chapter 26

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But why do we make inquiry into these points?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why do we both suffer and cause unnecessary delay?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Are we likely to find out by such a course as this what means we are to use for cleansing the conscience of the recipient, who does not know that the conscience of the giver is stained with guilt: whence the man is to receive faith who is unwittingly baptized by one that is faithless?—the question which Petilianus had proposed to himself to answer in my epistle, then going on to say anything else he pleased except what the matter in hand required.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: How often has he said, "If ignorant you were,"—as though I had said, what I never did say, that I was unacquainted with the conscience of him who baptized me.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And he seemed to have no other object in all that his evil-speaking mouth poured forth, except that he should appear to prove that I had not been ignorant of the misdeeds of those among whom I was baptized, and with whom I was associated in communion, understanding fully, it would seem, that ignorance did not convict me of guilt.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: See then that if I were ignorant, as he has repeated so often, beyond all doubt I should be innocent of all these crimes.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Whence therefore should I be cleansed, who am unacquainted with the conscience of him who gives but not in holiness, so that I may be least ensnared by his offenses?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Whence then should I receive faith, seeing that I was baptized unwittingly by one that was faithless?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For he has not repeated "If ignorant you were" so often without purpose, but simply to prevent my being reputed innocent, esteeming beyond all doubt that no man’s innocence is violated if he unwittingly receives his faith from one that is faithless, and is not acquainted with the stains on the conscience of him that gives, but not in holiness.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let him say, therefore, by what means such men are to be cleansed, whence they are to receive not guilt but faith.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But let him not deceive you.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let him not, while uttering much, say nothing; or rather, let him not say much while saying nothing.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Next, to urge a point which occurs to me, and must not be passed over,—if I am guilty because I have not been ignorant, to use his own phraseology, and I am proved not to have been ignorant, because I am an African, and already advanced in years, let him grant that the youths of other nations throughout the world are not guilty, who had no opportunity either from their race, or from that age you bring against me, of knowing the points that are laid to our charge, be they true, or be they false; and yet they, if they have fallen into your hands, are rebaptized without any considerations of such a kind.
